chapter.p: JudeвАЩs reverie was interrupted by the creak of footsteps ascending the stairs.
He whisked SueвАЩs clothing from the chair where it was drying, thrust it under the bed, and sat down to his book. Somebody knocked and opened the door immediately. It was the landlady.
вАЬO, I didnвАЩt know whether you was in or not, Mr. Fawley. I wanted to know if you would require supper. I see youвАЩve a young gentlemanвБ†вАФвАЭ
вАЬYes, maвАЩam. But I think I wonвАЩt come down tonight. Will you bring supper up on a tray, and IвАЩll have a cup of tea as well.вАЭ

It was JudeвАЩs custom to go downstairs to the kitchen, and eat his meals with the family, to save trouble. His landlady brought up the supper, however, on this occasion, and he took it from her at the door.

When she had descended he set the teapot on the hob, and drew out SueвАЩs clothes anew; but they were far from dry. A thick woollen gown, he found, held a deal of water. So he hung them up again, and enlarged his fire and mused as the steam from the garments went up the chimney.
Suddenly she said, вАЬJude!вАЭ
вАЬYes. All right. How do you feel now?вАЭ
вАЬBetter. Quite well. Why, I fell asleep, didnвАЩt I? What time is it? Not late surely?вАЭ
вАЬIt is past ten.вАЭ
вАЬIs it really? What shall I do!вАЭ she said, starting up.
вАЬStay where you are.вАЭ
вАЬYes; thatвАЩs what I want to do. But I donвАЩt know what they would say! And what will you do?вАЭ

вАЬI am going to sit here by the fire all night, and read. Tomorrow is Sunday, and I havenвАЩt to go out anywhere. Perhaps you will be saved a severe illness by resting there. DonвАЩt be frightened. IвАЩm all right. Look here, what I have got for you. Some supper.вАЭ

When she had sat upright she breathed plaintively and said, вАЬI do feel rather weak still. I thought I was well; and I ought not to be here, ought I?вАЭ But the supper fortified her somewhat, and when she had had some tea and had lain back again she was bright and cheerful.
The tea must have been green, or too long drawn, for she seemed preternaturally wakeful afterwards, though Jude, who had not taken any, began to feel heavy; till her conversation fixed his attention.
вАЬYou called me a creature of civilization, or something, didnвАЩt you?вАЭ she said, breaking a silence. вАЬIt was very odd you should have done that.вАЭ
вАЬWhy?вАЭ
вАЬWell, because it is provokingly wrong. I am a sort of negation of it.вАЭ
вАЬYou are very philosophical. вАШA negationвАЩ is profound talking.вАЭ
вАЬIs it? Do I strike you as being learned?вАЭ she asked, with a touch of raillery.
вАЬNoвБ†вАФnot learned. Only you donвАЩt talk quite like a girlвБ†вАФwell, a girl who has had no advantages.вАЭ

вАЬI have had advantages. I donвАЩt know Latin and Greek, though I know the grammars of those tongues. But I know most of the Greek and Latin classics through translations, and other books too. I read Lempri√®re, Catullus, Martial, Juvenal, Lucian, Beaumont and Fletcher, Boccaccio, Scarron, De Brant√іme, Sterne, De Foe, Smollett, Fielding, Shakespeare, the Bible, and other such; and found that all interest in the unwholesome part of those books ended with its mystery.вАЭ
вАЬYou have read more than I,вАЭ he said with a sigh. вАЬHow came you to read some of those queerer ones?вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ she said thoughtfully, вАЬit was by accident. My life has been entirely shaped by what people call a peculiarity in me. I have no fear of men, as such, nor of their books. I have mixed with themвБ†вАФone or two of them particularlyвБ†вАФalmost as one of their own sex. I mean I have not felt about them as most women are taught to feelвБ†вАФto be on their guard against attacks on their virtue; for no average manвБ†вАФno man short of a sensual savageвБ†вАФwill molest a woman by day or night, at home or abroad, unless she invites him. Until she says by a look вАШCome onвАЩ he is always afraid to, and if you never say it, or look it, he never comes. However, what I was going to say is that when I was eighteen I formed a friendly intimacy with an undergraduate at Christminster, and he taught me a great deal, and lent me books which I should never have got hold of otherwise.вАЭ
вАЬIs your friendship broken off?вАЭ
вАЬO yes. He died, poor fellow, two or three years after he had taken his degree and left Christminster.вАЭ
вАЬYou saw a good deal of him, I suppose?вАЭ

вАЬYes. We used to go about togetherвБ†вАФon walking tours, reading tours, and things of that sortвБ†вАФlike two men almost. He asked me to live with him, and I agreed to by letter. But when I joined him in London I found he meant a different thing from what I meant. He wanted me to be his mistress, in fact, but I wasnвАЩt in love with himвБ†вАФand on my saying I should go away if he didnвАЩt agree to my plan, he did so. We shared a sitting-room for fifteen months; and he became a leader-writer for one of the great London dailies; till he was taken ill, and had to go abroad. He said I was breaking his heart by holding out against him so long at such close quarters; he could never have believed it of woman. I might play that game once too often, he said. He came home merely to die. His death caused a terrible remorse in me for my crueltyвБ†вАФthough I hope he died of consumption and not of me entirely. I went down to Sandbourne to his funeral, and was his only mourner. He left me a little moneyвБ†вАФbecause I broke his heart, I suppose. ThatвАЩs how men areвБ†вАФso much better than women!вАЭ
вАЬGood heavens!вБ†вАФwhat did you do then?вАЭ
вАЬAhвБ†вАФnow you are angry with me!вАЭ she said, a contralto note of tragedy coming suddenly into her silvery voice. вАЬI wouldnвАЩt have told you if I had known!вАЭ
вАЬNo, I am not. Tell me all.вАЭ

вАЬWell, I invested his money, poor fellow, in a bubble scheme, and lost it. I lived about London by myself for some time, and then I returned to Christminster, as my fatherвБ†вАФwho was also in London, and had started as an art metalworker near Long-AcreвБ†вАФwouldnвАЩt have me back; and I got that occupation in the artist-shop where you found me.вБ† вБ†вА¶ I said you didnвАЩt know how bad I was!вАЭ

Jude looked round upon the armchair and its occupant, as if to read more carefully the creature he had given shelter to. His voice trembled as he said: вАЬHowever you have lived, Sue, I believe you are as innocent as you are unconventional!вАЭ
вАЬI am not particularly innocent, as you see, now that I have

p.span: From that blank lay-figure your fancy draped,вАЩ вАЭ

chapter.p: said she, with an ostensible sneer, though he could hear that she was brimming with tears. вАЬBut I have never yielded myself to any lover, if thatвАЩs what you mean! I have remained as I began.вАЭ
вАЬI quite believe you. But some women would not have remained as they began.вАЭ

вАЬPerhaps not. Better women would not. People say I must be cold-naturedвБ†вАФsexlessвБ†вАФon account of it. But I wonвАЩt have it! Some of the most passionately erotic poets have been the most self-contained in their daily lives.вАЭ
вАЬHave you told Mr. Phillotson about this University-scholar-friend?вАЭ
вАЬYesвБ†вАФlong ago. I have never made any secret of it to anybody.вАЭ
вАЬWhat did he say?вАЭ
вАЬHe did not pass any criticismвБ†вАФonly said I was everything to him, whatever I did; and things like that.вАЭ
Jude felt much depressed; she seemed to get further and further away from him with her strange ways and curious unconsciousness of gender.

вАЬArenвАЩt you really vexed with me, dear Jude?вАЭ she suddenly asked, in a voice of such extraordinary tenderness that it hardly seemed to come from the same woman who had just told her story so lightly. вАЬI would rather offend anybody in the world than you, I think!вАЭ
вАЬI donвАЩt know whether I am vexed or not. I know I care very much about you!вАЭ
вАЬI care as much for you as for anybody I ever met.вАЭ
вАЬYou donвАЩt care more! There, I ought not to say that. DonвАЩt answer it!вАЭ
There was another long silence. He felt that she was treating him cruelly, though he could not quite say in what way. Her very helplessness seemed to make her so much stronger than he.

вАЬI am awfully ignorant on general matters, although I have worked so hard,вАЭ he said, to turn the subject. вАЬI am absorbed in Theology, you know. And what do you think I should be doing just about now, if you werenвАЩt here? I should be saying my evening prayers. I suppose you wouldnвАЩt likeвБ†вАФвАЭ
вАЬO no, no,вАЭ she answered, вАЬI would rather not, if you donвАЩt mind. I should seem soвБ†вАФsuch a hypocrite.вАЭ
вАЬI thought you wouldnвАЩt join, so I didnвАЩt propose it. You must remember that I hope to be a useful minister some day.вАЭ
вАЬTo be ordained, I think you said?вАЭ
вАЬYes.вАЭ
вАЬThen you havenвАЩt given up the idea?вБ†вАФI thought that perhaps you had by this time.вАЭ
вАЬOf course not. I fondly thought at first that you felt as I do about that, as you were so mixed up in Christminster Anglicanism. And Mr. PhillotsonвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬI have no respect for Christminster whatever, except, in a qualified degree, on its intellectual side,вАЭ said Sue Bridehead earnestly. вАЬMy friend I spoke of took that out of me. He was the most irreligious man I ever knew, and the most moral. And intellect at Christminster is new wine in old bottles. The medievalism of Christminster must go, be sloughed off, or Christminster itself will have to go. To be sure, at times one couldnвАЩt help having a sneaking liking for the traditions of the old faith, as preserved by a section of the thinkers there in touching and simple sincerity; but when I was in my saddest, rightest mind I always felt,

p.span: вАШO ghastly glories of saints, dead limbs of gibbeted Gods!вАЩ вАЭвБ† вБ†вА¶

chapter.p: вАЬSue, you are not a good friend of mine to talk like that!вАЭ
вАЬThen I wonвАЩt, dear Jude!вАЭ The emotional throat-note had come back, and she turned her face away.
вАЬI still think Christminster has much that is glorious; though I was resentful because I couldnвАЩt get there.вАЭ He spoke gently, and resisted his impulse to pique her on to tears.

вАЬIt is an ignorant place, except as to the townspeople, artizans, drunkards, and paupers,вАЭ she said, perverse still at his differing from her. вАЬThey see life as it is, of course; but few of the people in the colleges do. You prove it in your own person. You are one of the very men Christminster was intended for when the colleges were founded; a man with a passion for learning, but no money, or opportunities, or friends. But you were elbowed off the pavement by the millionairesвАЩ sons.вАЭ
вАЬWell, I can do without what it confers. I care for something higher.вАЭ

вАЬAnd I for something broader, truer,вАЭ she insisted. вАЬAt present intellect in Christminster is pushing one way, and religion the other; and so they stand stock-still, like two rams butting each other.вАЭ
вАЬWhat would Mr. PhillotsonвБ†вАФвАЭ
вАЬIt is a place full of fetishists and ghost-seers!вАЭ

He noticed that whenever he tried to speak of the schoolmaster she turned the conversation to some generalizations about the offending University. Jude was extremely, morbidly, curious about her life as PhillotsonвАЩs prot√©g√©e and betrothed; yet she would not enlighten him.
вАЬWell, thatвАЩs just what I am, too,вАЭ he said. вАЬI am fearful of life, spectre-seeing always.вАЭ
вАЬBut you are good and dear!вАЭ she murmured.
His heart bumped, and he made no reply.

вАЬYou are in the Tractarian stage just now, are you not?вАЭ she added, putting on flippancy to hide real feeling, a common trick with her. вАЬLet me seeвБ†вАФwhen was I there?вБ†вАФIn the year eighteen hundred andвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬThereвАЩs a sarcasm in that which is rather unpleasant to me, Sue. Now will you do what I want you to? At this time I read a chapter, and then say prayers, as I told you. Now will you concentrate your attention on any book of these you like, and sit with your back to me, and leave me to my custom? You are sure you wonвАЩt join me?вАЭ
вАЬIвАЩll look at you.вАЭ
вАЬNo. DonвАЩt tease, Sue!вАЭ

вАЬVery wellвБ†вАФIвАЩll do just as you bid me, and I wonвАЩt vex you, Jude,вАЭ she replied, in the tone of a child who was going to be good forever after, turning her back upon him accordingly. A small Bible other than the one he was using lay near her, and during his retreat she took it up, and turned over the leaves.
вАЬJude,вАЭ she said brightly, when he had finished and come back to her; вАЬwill you let me make you a new New Testament, like the one I made for myself at Christminster?вАЭ
вАЬO yes. How was that made?вАЭ

вАЬI altered my old one by cutting up all the Epistles and Gospels into separate brochures, and rearranging them in chronological order as written, beginning the book with Thessalonians, following on with the Epistles, and putting the Gospels much further on. Then I had the volume rebound. My University friend Mr.вБ†вАФbut never mind his name, poor boyвБ†вАФsaid it was an excellent idea. I know that reading it afterwards made it twice as interesting as before, and twice as understandable.вАЭ
вАЬHвАЩm!вАЭ said Jude, with a sense of sacrilege.

вАЬAnd what a literary enormity this is,вАЭ she said, as she glanced into the pages of SolomonвАЩs Song. вАЬI mean the synopsis at the head of each chapter, explaining away the real nature of that rhapsody. You neednвАЩt be alarmed: nobody claims inspiration for the chapter headings. Indeed, many divines treat them with contempt. It seems the drollest thing to think of the four-and-twenty elders, or bishops, or whatever number they were, sitting with long faces and writing down such stuff.вАЭ
Jude looked pained. вАЬYou are quite Voltairean!вАЭ he murmured.

вАЬIndeed? Then I wonвАЩt say any more, except that people have no right to falsify the Bible! I hate such humbug as could attempt to plaster over with ecclesiastical abstractions such ecstatic, natural, human love as lies in that great and passionate song!вАЭ Her speech had grown spirited, and almost petulant at his rebuke, and her eyes moist. вАЬI wish I had a friend here to support me; but nobody is ever on my side!вАЭ
вАЬBut, my dear Sue, my very dear Sue, I am not against you!вАЭ he said, taking her hand, and surprised at her introducing personal feeling into mere argument.

вАЬYes you are, yes you are!вАЭ she cried, turning away her face that he might not see her brimming eyes. вАЬYou are on the side of the people in the Training SchoolвБ†вАФat least you seem almost to be! What I insist on is, that to explain such verses as this: вАШWhither is thy beloved gone, O thou fairest among women?вАЩ by the note: вАШThe Church professeth her faithвАЩ is supremely ridiculous!вАЭ
вАЬWell then, let it be! You make such a personal matter of everything! I amвБ†вАФonly too inclined just now to apply the words profanely. You know you are fairest among women to me, come to that!вАЭ

вАЬBut you are not to say it now!вАЭ Sue replied, her voice changing to its softest note of severity. Then their eyes met, and they shook hands like cronies in a tavern, and Jude saw the absurdity of quarrelling on such a hypothetical subject, and she the silliness of crying about what was written in an old book like the Bible.

вАЬI wonвАЩt disturb your convictionsвБ†вАФI really wonвАЩt!вАЭ she went on soothingly, for now he was rather more ruffled than she. вАЬBut I did want and long to ennoble some man to high aims; and when I saw you, and knew you wanted to be my comrade, IвБ†вАФshall I confess it?вБ†вАФthought that man might be you. But you take so much tradition on trust that I donвАЩt know what to say.вАЭ
вАЬWell, dear; I suppose one must take some things on trust. Life isnвАЩt long enough to work out everything in Euclid problems before you believe it. I take Christianity.вАЭ
вАЬWell, perhaps you might take something worse.вАЭ
вАЬIndeed I might. Perhaps I have done so!вАЭ He thought of Arabella.

вАЬI wonвАЩt ask what, because we are going to be very nice with each other, arenвАЩt we, and never, never, vex each other any more?вАЭ She looked up trustfully, and her voice seemed trying to nestle in his breast.
вАЬI shall always care for you!вАЭ said Jude.
вАЬAnd I for you. Because you are single-hearted, and forgiving to your faulty and tiresome little Sue!вАЭ

He looked away, for that epicene tenderness of hers was too harrowing. Was it that which had broken the heart of the poor leader-writer; and was he to be the next one?вБ† вБ†вА¶ But Sue was so dear!вБ† вБ†вА¶ If he could only get over the sense of her sex, as she seemed to be able to do so easily of his, what a comrade she would make; for their difference of opinion on conjectural subjects only drew them closer together on matters of daily human experience. She was nearer to him than any other woman he had ever met, and he could scarcely believe that time, creed, or absence, would ever divide him from her.

But his grief at her incredulities returned. They sat on till she fell asleep again, and he nodded in his chair likewise. Whenever he aroused himself he turned her things, and made up the fire anew. About six oвАЩclock he awoke completely, and lighting a candle, found that her clothes were dry. Her chair being a far more comfortable one than his she still slept on inside his greatcoat, looking warm as a new bun and boyish as a Ganymedes. Placing the garments by her and touching her on the shoulder he went downstairs, and washed himself by starlight in the yard.
